subtopic_detail: Tubal ectopic pregnancy single-dose methotrexate criteria
stem:
You are the gynaecology registrar reviewing a 28-year-old G2P0 woman with 6 weeks 2 days amenorrhoea, mild left-sided abdominal pain for 2 days, and one episode of light brown vaginal spotting today. Her observations are stable. She had a normal full blood examination, baseline liver and renal function are normal, and she has no known medical comorbidity, no breastfeeding, no immunosuppression and no known peptic ulcer disease.

She is rhesus D positive and has full insight into the diagnosis and treatment options. She has a partner and can attend follow-up scans and blood tests reliably for at least the next two weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 78, BP 122/72, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, mild left iliac fossa tenderness, no rebound or guarding, no cervical motion tenderness, closed cervical os with no products of conception
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 1850 IU/L; transvaginal ultrasound: empty uterus, 22 mm left adnexal mass with no foetal cardiac activity, no free fluid in pouch of Douglas; Hb 128, normal LFTs and renal function

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Administer single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg per square metre body surface area today, arrange beta-hCG measurement on days 4 and 7, and counsel on avoidance of alcohol, folic acid, NSAIDs and conception for 3 months
options.rationale: She meets criteria for single-dose methotrexate (stable, hCG below 5000, mass under 35 mm, no cardiac activity, normal bloods, reliable follow-up); 15% fall day 4 to day 7 indicates success. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Proceed to laparoscopic salpingectomy this evening regardless of haemodynamic stability because all tubal ectopic pregnancies require definitive surgical management to reduce future recurrence and persistent trophoblast risk in the contralateral fallopian tube
options.rationale: Stable women meeting criteria for medical management can be offered methotrexate; surgery is not mandatory for every tubal ectopic. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Adopt expectant management with weekly beta-hCG monitoring until resolution because the beta-hCG is below 1000 and the ectopic mass measures less than 30 mm on transvaginal ultrasound with no haemorrhage, avoiding the toxicity of methotrexate completely
options.rationale: Expectant management is for falling hCG below 1000 with no symptoms; this woman has rising hCG with symptoms and a visible mass needing active management. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Insert misoprostol 800 micrograms per vagina now for medical termination of the ectopic pregnancy, with mifepristone 200 mg orally 48 hours later, and arrange beta-hCG monitoring weekly until resolution to baseline
options.rationale: Misoprostol and mifepristone are for intrauterine miscarriage; methotrexate is the medical option for tubal ectopic pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Admit for observation with intravenous fluids, fasting from midnight, and laparoscopic salpingostomy in the morning if there is no clinical improvement overnight, with intraoperative methotrexate flush if any persistent trophoblastic tissue is identified
options.rationale: Salpingostomy with intraoperative methotrexate is not standard practice; salpingectomy is preferred when surgery is needed and patient meets medical criteria. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Single-dose methotrexate is appropriate for selected stable women with tubal ectopic pregnancy: beta-hCG below 5000 (some centres below 3500), adnexal mass under 35 mm, no foetal cardiac activity, no free fluid suggesting rupture, normal full blood examination, liver and renal function, and reliable follow-up. Dosing is methotrexate 50 mg per square metre intramuscularly with beta-hCG on days 4 and 7; a fall of at least 15% between those days indicates response. The woman avoids folic acid, NSAIDs, alcohol and conception for 3 months. Surgical salpingectomy is preferred when criteria are not met.
key_points: MTX single-dose criteria: hCG under 5000, mass under 35 mm, no cardiac activity, stable
Monitor hCG on day 4 and day 7; aim for 15% fall between these days
Avoid folate, NSAIDs, alcohol and conception for 3 months
explanation.pearls: If hCG fall between days 4 and 7 is less than 15%, give a second dose of methotrexate; persistent rise or new symptoms triggers surgical management.

stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman with 18 months of primary subfertility. She has cycles of 35 to 90 days, mild hirsutism, a BMI of 29, and ultrasound showing polycystic ovarian morphology bilaterally. Her partner's semen analysis is normal, her tubes are patent on hysterosalpingography, day 21 progesterone is consistently low, thyroid function and prolactin are normal, and she has had ongoing dietary support with structured exercise for 12 months without conceiving. She is non-smoker, non-diabetic and rubella immune. She has no contraindications to letrozole or clomiphene.

data_table.Examination: Mild upper-lip and chin hirsutism, no acanthosis nigricans, normal pelvic exam
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic ultrasound polycystic ovaries bilaterally, no endometrial pathology; HSG patent tubes; partner semen analysis normal; HbA1c 5.4%, TSH 1.8, prolactin normal, fasting cholesterol panel normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological therapy for ovulation induction?

options.text: Commence subcutaneous follicle-stimulating hormone gonadotropin injections from day 2 of the cycle with serial transvaginal ultrasound follicle tracking and a triggering dose of human chorionic gonadotropin once the lead follicle reaches 18 mm in diameter
options.rationale: Gonadotropins are second or third line after letrozole and clomiphene due to higher cost, multiple pregnancy risk and ovarian hyperstimulation; not first-line in PCOS. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023 (Monash))

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg orally daily from cycle days 2 to 6 with serial follicle tracking and timed intercourse, escalating to 5 mg in the next cycle if no ovulation is achieved, with maximum dose 7.5 mg daily for a maximum of 5 to 6 cycles
options.rationale: Letrozole is the first-line ovulation induction agent in PCOS per the 2023 International PCOS Guideline, with higher live birth rates than clomiphene and lower multiple pregnancy risk. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023 (Monash))

options.text: Metformin 500 mg three times daily for 6 months as monotherapy, with avoidance of clomiphene or letrozole because metformin alone in normoglycaemic women with polycystic ovarian syndrome is sufficient to restore ovulation and achieve conception
options.rationale: Metformin alone has lower live birth rates than letrozole or clomiphene; it is an adjunct, not first-line monotherapy for ovulation induction. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023 (Monash))

options.text: Refer immediately for in vitro fertilisation with intracytoplasmic sperm injection as first-line management of subfertility in polycystic ovarian syndrome to maximise success rates given the long duration of infertility and the age of the woman
options.rationale: IVF is third-line in PCOS after ovulation induction failure; it is not first-line for an otherwise straightforward anovulatory presentation at age 28. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023 (Monash))

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg orally daily from cycle days 2 to 6 with serial follicle tracking and timed intercourse, escalating to 100 mg in the next cycle if anovulatory, with up to 6 ovulatory cycles before escalating to second-line therapy
options.rationale: Clomiphene is now second-line behind letrozole due to lower live birth rates and higher multiple pregnancy rates in PCOS. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023 (Monash))

explanation.summary: The 2023 International PCOS Guideline (led by Monash University and endorsed in Australia) recommends letrozole as the first-line pharmacological agent for ovulation induction in polycystic ovarian syndrome with anovulatory subfertility, given superior live birth rates and lower multiple pregnancy risk compared with clomiphene. Dosing starts at 2.5 mg from day 2 to 6 with escalation if no ovulation, to a maximum of 7.5 mg daily, for 5 to 6 ovulatory cycles. Metformin is an adjunct, particularly in higher BMI or insulin-resistant patients, and clomiphene is now considered second-line.
key_points: Letrozole 2.5 mg days 2-6 is first-line in PCOS (2023 guideline)
Higher live birth and lower multiple pregnancy vs clomiphene
Metformin is adjunct; IVF is third-line after failed ovulation induction
explanation.pearls: Letrozole is off-label for ovulation induction in some jurisdictions but is supported by the international PCOS guideline and Monash IVF Australian practice; teratogenicity concerns have been refuted in well-conducted trials.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum levonorgestrel IUD 4 weeks postnatal in a breastfeeding woman
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 32-year-old G3P3 woman 4 weeks following an uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery of a term healthy infant. She is exclusively breastfeeding, has resumed sexual activity this week using condoms, and explicitly wants long-acting reversible contraception with a 5-year horizon and minimal day-to-day involvement. She has no history of pelvic infection, no abnormalities of the uterus on her recent postnatal examination, and is happy with vaginal insertion. She has no contraindications to progestogens and no migraine with aura. Her partner is supportive and she has age-appropriate completed family discussions.

data_table.Examination: Uterus well involuted, no tenderness, normal-looking cervix on speculum, lochia minimal, perineum healed
data_table.Investigations: Hb 132, postnatal urinalysis normal, no current STI symptoms

lead_in: Which contraceptive option is the most appropriate to offer at this consultation?

options.text: Recommend continued lactational amenorrhoea method alone for the first 6 months postpartum with exclusive breastfeeding and amenorrhoea, deferring all other contraceptive methods until weaning is established and a return to the GP at that time
options.rationale: LAM efficacy declines with mixed feeding and time and is not appropriate when the woman explicitly wants long-acting reversible contraception. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Postpartum Contraception)

options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill containing ethinyloestradiol 30 micrograms with levonorgestrel 150 micrograms today with quick-start instructions, plus condoms for 7 days as additional precaution while the pill becomes effective
options.rationale: COCP is restricted under 6 weeks postpartum and is category 4 in breastfeeding women under 6 weeks postpartum due to VTE and lactation suppression. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Postpartum Contraception)

options.text: Offer levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device insertion today as outpatient procedure in the practice, confirm she is not at risk of current pregnancy, provide written information and arrange a 6-week post-insertion check by phone or in person
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel IUD can be safely inserted from 4 weeks postpartum, is category 1 in breastfeeding women, and provides 5 years of highly effective long-acting reversible contraception. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Postpartum Contraception)

options.text: Refer for laparoscopic tubal ligation as the most appropriate definitive long-acting contraception for a multiparous woman who states she has completed her family, with day-surgery booking and preoperative counselling on regret and reversibility
options.rationale: Permanent sterilisation is not the most appropriate option when reversible long-acting reversible contraception meets her stated 5-year horizon and avoids surgical risks. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Postpartum Contraception)

options.text: Prescribe progestogen-only oral contraceptive desogestrel 75 micrograms taken at the same time each day with a 12-hour late window for missed doses, with condom backup for the first 48 hours after starting and a 6-week phone review
options.rationale: POP is safe in breastfeeding but does not meet her stated preference for long-acting reversible contraception; LARC is more effective and lower-maintenance. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Postpartum Contraception)

explanation.summary: From 4 weeks postpartum, the levonorgestrel intrauterine device can be inserted safely in breastfeeding women and is category 1 by the WHO and Family Planning Australia. It provides 5 years of highly effective long-acting reversible contraception with minimal maintenance and the added benefit of reducing menstrual loss over time. Combined oral contraceptives are restricted in breastfeeding women under 6 weeks postpartum because of venous thromboembolism risk and theoretical lactation suppression. Etonogestrel implant and progestogen-only pill are safe alternatives if IUD is declined or contraindicated.
key_points: LNG-IUD from 4 weeks postpartum is category 1 in breastfeeding women
COCP is restricted under 6 weeks postpartum and avoided if breastfeeding under 6 weeks
Etonogestrel implant and POP are safe alternatives at any postnatal time
explanation.pearls: Immediate post-placental IUD insertion (within 10 minutes of delivery) is increasingly offered in Australian tertiary centres; expulsion rates are higher than interval insertion but acceptability and continuation are improved.

subtopic_detail: VBAC counselling for prior single low transverse caesarean
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 33-year-old G2P1 woman at 36 weeks gestation in a tertiary centre antenatal clinic for mode of birth discussion. Her first pregnancy was complicated by breech presentation managed with an elective lower segment caesarean at 39 weeks; the operation was uncomplicated and recovery uneventful. The current pregnancy has been uncomplicated, the foetus is cephalic, growth is appropriate, the placenta is normally sited, and there are no contraindications to vaginal birth. She wants to understand her options for this delivery and is open to either elective repeat caesarean or planned vaginal birth after caesarean.

data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height appropriate, cephalic presentation, well engaged, no scar tenderness, uterus soft and non-irritable
data_table.Investigations: Recent growth ultrasound at 34 weeks with estimated foetal weight on the 55th centile, anterior placenta away from scar, normal liquor volume; bloods unremarkable; CTG normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and management plan?

options.text: Recommend mandatory elective repeat lower segment caesarean section at 39 plus 0 weeks gestation to all women with a previous caesarean delivery to avoid the risks of uterine rupture and emergency caesarean associated with planned vaginal birth after caesarean
options.rationale: Mandatory repeat caesarean is not consistent with shared decision-making; VBAC is offered to suitable women after individualised counselling about benefits and risks. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth After Caesarean)

options.text: Offer planned home waterbirth supervised by midwifery group practice for vaginal birth after caesarean given her low-risk antenatal course and previous normal recovery from caesarean, with ambulance transfer arrangement to the tertiary hospital
options.rationale: VBAC should be conducted in a centre with continuous CTG and immediate access to theatre; home water birth is not appropriate due to rupture risk. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth After Caesarean)

options.text: Plan induction of labour with prostaglandin E2 vaginal gel at 38 weeks gestation in all women planning vaginal birth after caesarean to optimise success rates by avoiding the risks associated with spontaneous post-term labour or a prolonged latent phase
options.rationale: Prostaglandins for cervical ripening are contraindicated in VBAC due to a fivefold higher rupture risk; induction of VBAC, if any, is by mechanical methods or oxytocin. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth After Caesarean)

options.text: Discuss planned vaginal birth after caesarean with quoted success rate of 72 to 75% in appropriate candidates and uterine rupture risk of 0.5%, plan for spontaneous labour with continuous CTG monitoring and IV access at a centre with immediate access to theatre
options.rationale: VBAC is offered with quoted success rate around 72-75% and rupture risk of 0.5%, with continuous CTG and immediate theatre access; this is shared-decision counselling. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth After Caesarean)

options.text: Offer elective repeat caesarean section at 37 plus 0 weeks gestation given the maternal preference for a planned approach and the avoidance of intrapartum monitoring, with antenatal corticosteroids for foetal lung maturity prior to the procedure
options.rationale: Elective repeat caesarean is timed at 39 plus 0 weeks; earlier delivery exposes the neonate to respiratory morbidity and antenatal corticosteroids are not standard at term. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth After Caesarean)

explanation.summary: Planned vaginal birth after a single previous low transverse caesarean is appropriate for selected women without absolute contraindications. RANZCOG counselling quotes a success rate of 72-75% and a uterine rupture rate of approximately 0.5%. VBAC should be conducted in a facility with continuous intrapartum CTG monitoring, IV access, and immediate access to theatre and blood. Spontaneous labour is preferred over induction; if induction is required, mechanical methods or low-dose oxytocin are used rather than prostaglandins, which carry a fivefold higher rupture risk. Elective repeat caesarean is timed at 39 plus 0 weeks.
key_points: VBAC success quoted as 72-75% in suitable candidates
Uterine rupture risk approximately 0.5%; continuous CTG and immediate theatre access required
Avoid prostaglandins for induction in VBAC; if induced, use mechanical methods or oxytocin
explanation.pearls: Two previous caesareans, classical scar, prior uterine rupture, induction at unfavourable cervix, and short inter-pregnancy interval each substantially raise rupture risk and may shift recommendation toward elective repeat caesarean.

sources.label: NSW Health: Vaginal Birth After Caesarean

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis and BP control
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar called to the assessment unit to see a 34-year-old G1P0 woman at 34 weeks gestation with a 3-day history of escalating headache, blurred vision and right upper quadrant pain. She has had antenatal care including a normal morphology scan and her blood pressure has been climbing over the last 2 weeks. She has had no seizure activity.

Her foetus is reactive on CTG with normal liquor and growth on recent ultrasound. She has no allergies, no asthma and no significant past history. The neonatal team has been contacted and a single antenatal corticosteroid course was given at 32 weeks for prior threatened preterm labour.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/108 confirmed on repeat in two arms, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, reflexes 3+ with sustained clonus at the right ankle
data_table.Examination: Right upper quadrant tenderness, no peripheral oedema, no neurology focal deficit, foetus active, fundal height appropriate
data_table.Investigations: Urine PCR 350 mg/mmol, Hb 116, platelets 92, ALT 110, urate 0.42, creatinine 88, LDH 540, normal coags, CTG reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence oral nifedipine slow-release 30 mg twice daily, observe for 6 hours in the assessment unit and admit if her blood pressure does not respond to two doses, with arrangement of repeat bloods and CTG monitoring in the morning
options.rationale: Slow-release nifedipine is not for acute severe hypertension in pregnancy; IV labetalol or hydralazine (or immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg oral) are the appropriate agents. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange immediate emergency caesarean section without prior maternal stabilisation, given the severe blood pressure readings, the symptoms of headache and right upper quadrant pain, and the platelet count of 92 with deranged liver function
options.rationale: Maternal stabilisation with MgSO4 and antihypertensive must precede delivery in severe pre-eclampsia unless there is an immediate threat to life. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer intravenous phenytoin loading dose 18 mg/kg over 30 minutes for seizure prophylaxis with continuous cardiac monitoring, and commence labetalol infusion at 20 mg/hour to control her blood pressure to a target below 160/110
options.rationale: Phenytoin is inferior to magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis in pre-eclampsia (Magpie and Eclampsia Trial); MgSO4 is the agent of choice. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence diazepam 10 mg intravenously now for seizure prophylaxis, continue antenatal corticosteroids with a repeat dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscularly, and admit to the ward for serial maternal observations and 4-hourly bloods
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not for seizure prophylaxis in pre-eclampsia; MgSO4 plus antihypertensive plus delivery planning is correct. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading dose over 15 minutes then 1 g/hour infusion for seizure prophylaxis, give IV labetalol 20 mg bolus repeated as needed for blood pressure target below 160/110, admit to the high-dependency unit and plan delivery within 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia at 34 weeks is managed with MgSO4 seizure prophylaxis (4 g load then 1 g/hour), IV antihypertensive (labetalol or hydralazine), HDU admission, and planned delivery within 24-48 hours. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia (BP at or above 160/110 with end-organ involvement) at 34 weeks gestation is managed with maternal stabilisation followed by planned delivery within 24-48 hours. Magnesium sulfate is the agent of choice for seizure prophylaxis (4 g IV loading dose over 15 minutes followed by 1 g/hour infusion), with monitoring of reflexes and urine output. IV labetalol bolus (or hydralazine, or immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg orally) is used for acute severe hypertension targeting BP below 160/110 mmHg. The Magpie and Eclampsia trials demonstrated MgSO4 superiority over phenytoin and diazepam.
key_points: MgSO4 4 g IV load then 1 g/hour (Magpie trial)
IV labetalol or hydralazine for acute severe BP, target below 160/110
Plan delivery within 24-48 hours after maternal stabilisation
explanation.pearls: MgSO4 toxicity is monitored clinically: loss of patellar reflex is the earliest sign and requires infusion cessation; calcium gluconate 1 g IV is the antidote for severe respiratory or cardiac toxicity.
